---
title: "Statistics"
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: false
    slide-level: 2
---

## Motivation

**Question: are NBA Players really taller than most people?**

![](../../assets/nba_players_are_tall.png){fig-align="center" .r-stretch}


## Population

**Population** (مجتمع إحصائي): The object which the research intends to generalize its results towards.

- People: like every person living in a specific neighborhood
- Things: like every lightbulb produced in a factory today

**Sample** (عينة): is subset of *Population*. Our sample is NBA Players (at some point in time).

![](../../assets/population_sample.png){fig-align="center" .r-stretch}


## Thinking in levels about population and sample

In reality, we don't always have access to all population. Hence, it is helpful to think in levels, The smallest of which is the **Participant** (مشارك):

![](../../assets/population_sampling_reality.png){fig-align="center" .r-stretch}

::: {.notes}

In research, these terms describe a "funnel" that narrows down from a massive group of people to the specific individuals being studied.

1. **Population:** The entire broad group of people that your research results are intended to describe (e.g., "all adults with Type 2 diabetes").
2. **Target Population:** The specific subset of the population that meets your formal "eligibility rules" or sampling criteria (e.g., "adults with Type 2 diabetes living in Canada, aged 40–60").
3. **Accessible Population:** The actual portion of the target population that the researcher can realistically reach and recruit from (e.g., "adults with Type 2 diabetes at three specific clinics in Toronto").
4. **Sample:** The small group of individuals chosen from the accessible population who actually take part in the study.
5. **Subject, Participant, or Informant:** A single individual within the sample.

:::


## Variables

**Variables** (متغيرات) are the shared attributes of the subjects. They may be:

- **Numerical** (عددي) weight, temperature, or duration.
- **Categorical** (فئوي) "Success/Failure," color, or brand.

Can you distinguish them the following tabl e?

|     | species | island    | bill_length_mm | bill_depth_mm | flipper_length_mm | body_mass_g | sex    |
| --- | ------- | --------- | -------------- | ------------- | ----------------- | ----------- | ------ |
| 0   | Adelie  | Torgersen | 39.1           | 18.7          | 181.0             | 3750.0      | Male   |
| 1   | Adelie  | Torgersen | 39.5           | 17.4          | 186.0             | 3800.0      | Female |
| 2   | Adelie  | Torgersen | 40.3           | 18.0          | 195.0             | 3250.0      | Female |
| 3   | Adelie  | Torgersen | NaN            | NaN           | NaN               | NaN         | NaN    |
| 4   | Adelie  | Torgersen | 36.7           | 19.3          | 193.0             | 3450.0      | Female |

- Relevance is key. Pick attributes that you think might explian logical relationship to the conclusions you want to make.
- In our case, we are looking at the `height` variable.

## Statistics

**Statistics** (إحصاءات) is the study of how best to collect, analyze, and draw conclusions from data.

**Descriptive Statistics** (إحصاء وصفي) summarizes qualities of a group (of people or things):

1. **Statistic / Parameter** (إحصاءة) (mean, median, variance, range, ..etc.)  
2. **Graph / Plot** (رسم بياني) (points, lines, colors, size, text, ..etc.)  

In both cases, we are interested in **multipe** statistics and **multiple** graphs, before we move on to making conclusions.

::: {.notes}
- Numerical. It sums up variables across subjects to describe them in general.
- Visual. They simplify the data by doing local statistics (e.g., bins of bar plot).
:::

## Inferentail Statistics

**Inferential Statistics** (احصاء استدلالي) goes from description to **drawing conclusions**.

![](../assets/inferential_statistics.png){fig-align="center" .r-stretch}

At its heart, statistics is the bridge between raw observation and useful information.

## Lab

> "involve me and I remember"
